speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. The survivor is Mr. Solly Irving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Irving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irving. Sorry. [LAUGHTER] Before the tape break, you were talking about the liquidation of the Ryki ghetto and that your father bribed a German soldier. Your other sisters, what happened to your family in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, according to the guy who-- the farmer that went with the house accountant to town-- he told me he saw my father walking in line with my older sister. He saw my mother in another line with my other sisters. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they liquidated the ghetto, then the sexes were split then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily, not necessarily. Those who were able to walk, walked. And those who weren't-- obviously, my mother must have been maybe even in one of the carts with my younger sister. I don't know. Yeah. That's what he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- not then, I didn't know. But I've learned since that they were taken to Sobibor by train, and so Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to life inside the ghetto, were-- how did life change? Did you-- were you still be-- were you still able to carry on with your religious learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we did. Yes, as far as religious, yes, or whatever. If you went to hide and they taught other subjects, obviously, you carried on, not for very long. I don't know how long the ghetto was established-- how long it was in existence, rather, before we were all taken out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the food like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I  don't remember much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you and your sister left, then, the Ryki ghetto, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to this farm that my father told me to make for. And the woman-- obviously, the man wasn't there. He was taking-- he went Tschenstochau to Ryki, but the woman was there, and she showed us into-- into the place. And she gave us some food and told them that we shouldn't stay here. We should carry on somewhere else. But we managed to stay until the man came home. And the man came looking for us in one of the outhouses of the farm. And he told us the story. He saw my father and my mother. And he said he couldn't look after us. He couldn't hide us because his wife was of German descent. So he advised us not to stay too long, gave us some food, and he told us to make for some other farms. There were farms in the area established and the Jews-- some of the Jewish people-- were taken in as laborers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wandered around for a while, and I think it must have been all night. And the next morning, we came across another farm, and we saw people working on the farm that I recognized. And it-- it was the rabbi's wife and daughters were there and some other people, and mostly women.

speaker: interviewee
text: And obviously, they were gathered. Whether they were Poles or Germans, I'm not quite sure. They were gathered. And somebody suggested that I should lay low till at night. My-- my sister, being older, joined in with them, working on the farm, and talked to the other people. And they advised me that I should stay low. But at night, they would try and take me into one of the barracks on the farm where they lived. And they did. And after a while of being there, we heard the-- the owner of the farm. He called the Politz, you  know. The squire come in, and, oh, somebody must have seen me climb through a window and do that. And he came in looking for me, and I was under the bunks, hiding under the bunks. I was small, so I slid under the bunks. And he said, I know somebody is here. You  better come out. If you don't come out, I'll send the Alsatian dog after you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came out, explained who I was. He was sympathetic, and he told me to stay, try and find some work, and so forth. He must have known my father-in-law, because they were all in that area. He must have known.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually-- I think it was a day or so later-- we heard that he was summoned to come into the-- come into to the [? tour ?], which is the headquarters of the German headquarters area. And the-- the farm had to be judenrein. It's sort of to do away with the Jews. And the Jews were going to be taken away again. And I heard about this. So he was going with his horse and cart, [? Toshki, ?] and I managed to-- with my sister-- managed to climb on the back of it. He didn't see us. And we ran. And we jumped out and on halfway, and we carried on running somewhere else. And eventually, I turned up in Deblin. Deblin was the nearest town, and we had a distant cousin of my father's. And he was also in the Judenrat, and he gave us shelter. And a while after-- again, I don't remember how long it was-- then we heard there was the-- it was the ghetto was being liquidated again. And that-- although it's what he wants, there were some people left behind because there was a camp nearby. And there was also an Air Force Base nearby. They needed people to work. So they kept on keeping people behind. And that-- that was the second liquidation of Deblin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back a little bit, when you reached the farm, do you remember the names? You don't remember any of the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The names of the squire or anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.  No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And do you remember like the size of the farm or what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing? Or if it had animals on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: All farms-- no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And why were you hiding under the bunk when you were in the second-- when you went to the other farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was [INAUDIBLE] because I wasn't allowed to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you said that the women that worked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were mainly-- were mainly-- were mainly women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know I don't know. Unless, I can only assume that the farm in-- I mean, being-- Ryki was a small town, all right? Most were people, either farmers or dealing in farm goods, like grain, and so forth. And they must have known the squire. And he beginning, took in these people to try and save them. That's-- that's-- I can assume so. I'm not quite sure. He obviously needed laborers as well, so he took the people in to labor. One-- one, particularly, was the wife of the rabbinate-- we call him in Hebrew-- of our rabbi's-- rabbi's wife and his daughter, [? Ahel, ?] who was my godmother. I remember that I was told that she was my godmother. And then the-- the mother was quite an elderly woman already by then. But nevertheless, I mean, people had some influence with these squires, and they-- but they bribed them to take them in. We thought it was-- maybe they thought it was only going to be days or weeks before the war would be over, and they would go. So they were hiding out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would imagine happened to so many others. That's all I can assume.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Deblin, were you in-- when you went there first, did you go straight into the ghetto, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a ghetto, obviously. All Jews lived in the ghetto. So if I looked for somebody-- I can't even remember his name, even-- and I looked for-- for this cousin, a distant cousin that I have. I had to look for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what was that ghetto like in comparison to the Ryki ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much. Like all ghettos. Some did the-- I mean, we're not talking about Warsaw ghettos. Obviously, it was much different than all large ghettos. But these small ghettos, being in small towns, it was restricted, obviously, to go out. There were restrictions of what you could do, but it wasn't threatening-- life-threatening. People were surrounded after taken to a different works. Like this friend of mine, Monty Tabachnik, [? Moto ?] Tabachnik, who comes from Deblin. And he was much taller than me, and he still is. [LAUGHS] And he was taken. He was grabbed in-- in the center of the town and got him taken to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the Deblin ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not long, not long, could've been weeks, if that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, as a child, nothing-- absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you live with when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my distant cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't the luxury we have today, let's put it that way. I have a flat here with three bedrooms and two bathrooms. I'm sure that many, many families could have fitted in here. The-- we didn't have those facilities and those luxuries in those days. It might have been one or two rooms and everybody mucked in together. That's all it was. The toilet must have been on the outside, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about food? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? I don't remember much about food, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't leave. The town was surrounded again, and it was being liquidated. And remembering when my father was there trying to stay alive, we always had a little parcel ready, in case you had to run, to grab it and run. But it was a change of clothes, a bit of food. That's all-- that's all it was. I came running home from the center of the town. I heard that the Germans were coming again. My sister was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Deblin is where I stayed with my cousin, but I called it home. And I grabbed hold of her, and we opened a back window, and we jumped in the fields, in the back of it. And we jumped out, and we were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see other people running?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see. I was only concerned about myself and my sister. I held her by my hand. She was older, but-- and we ran. And suddenly, we heard calls, 'Halt!,' which means stop and being shot at. And somehow-- somehow, we came across a farmer who was on the fields. He grabbed a hold of me. I wiggled out. He grabbed a hold of my sister. And this is only seconds. It couldn't have been much longer, because they've been running after me. And I begged him to release her, and I tried to give him money. We always had some money on us. I don't know how much, or whatever-- had to give him. And he wouldn't release her. And he says, look, they're behind you. And with remembering that, I just pulled my sister again, and I ran out around a little forest in the distance. And I must have passed out. That's the only thing I can-- all I remember is being shot, fired at, and 'Halt!, Halt! Halt!' And when I got up, I saw nothing. Everything was just bare. I must have still been dazed. I lay there for a while and started walking in the forest, coming across little groups who obviously managed to do the same thing that I did. And you heard-- in the night, you heard the cries from the train-- it must have been near the train station-- the cries and the firing. And we kept on wandering. We kept on walking. And the only thing I could follow was the electric poles, or the telegraph poles. I didn't know where I was. And we wandered around for-- for a while-- for-- for a good many weeks like that. And came across little [INAUDIBLE] in groups. They wouldn't-- didn't want me. I was too young. Other groups didn't want me either, because the more people, the more-- more danger. And eventually, after a while, I heard there was a camp nearby. An aunt of mine was in it-- my father's sister, named Tzipporah. She was there. And a cousin of mine named Chaim was there, the one who jumped from the train. How they got to that camp, I don't know, but they were there. And this is nearby an airfield, an Air Force Base. And I knew which sort of direction they were coming from work or to work. And one day, I managed to-- to get near and lie in hiding till they passed through and joined-- and joined that group going back into-- into the camp. And my aunt-- I mean, the people-- most of the people knew me, because they were local people, all local, either Deblin or Ryki people. There were quite a few from Ryki in those days, in that camp. And then they contact my aunt and they told her. And then she hid me in her barrack, the women's barrack. There were barracks for women and barracks for men. She hid me. And then, eventually, there was a registration in the camp, and I stood up. There was quite a lot of children. And I was registered as one of the inmates in the camp. So I was-- I didn't have to hide anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just going back a little bit, when you were in the forest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what food did you eat? How did you manage to--

speaker: interviewee
text: You manage to find only from farms, whatever you could find. At night, to get into a farm and find some raw fruit, vegetables, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then going back to this camp, what were the conditions like inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't think they were too bad. It was a labor camp. It was near our hometown. It was only surrounded by barbed wire and with a few Germans, about. There was-- I remember the gate, a barbed wire gate. There were times that people were hanged for trying to escape, or whatever. But you could always manage to find some extra food, because the Poles used to come to the camp and try and sell you extra food. Those people had money. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about sleeping conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunks, mostly bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a mattress? Did you have-- was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Straw, it was mostly straw, just lay straw. Some were better than some of the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what clothes did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal clothes I had on. [CLEARS THROAT] Must have been in-- ruined in tatters, or whatever it was. I don't know. I don't remember much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other contact with your family members? Or did you see any other family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't have any. But many people knew me, and that's why it was easy for me to be [INAUDIBLE] by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you have their contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And that camp is near also a railroad line. The-- the main railroad line used to pass through just about the camp. And we saw many at the train stop. One day, they took some people out from the camp to unload some clothes. They brought some clothing from the wagons. The people were taken in. You see the barbed wires on the little-- on the windows. And there were times when-- one occasion, when somebody found little notes and the names of the people's clothing they brought into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave this camp and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started work, I mean, in that camp, eventually being registered as a-- first, I started. I was doing a little bit of gardening there. And then they needed some people on the airfields to unload cement. And we always tried to register as older to what we were, so more chance of survival. Once you were in a group of workers, you have to give yourself the food. What the foods were like, I don't remember. But it wasn't very much. But nevertheless, you-- you-- you managed to survive, you know. And I was taken to the camps, and I overheard a group trying to-- to escape. And each one had to find their own firearms. But before that, I was working in coal mine-- not in coal mines, working in coal piles. They were unloading-- unloading coal or cement or helping bombs. It was an airfield at an Air Force Base. So therefore, people were needed. One day, a plane came down on fire. And some of the-- don't know exactly how many-- they ran forwards and kicked the-- the cockpit open and saved this pilot, a German pilot, obviously. He was sent for recuperation. And when he came back, he came back with some goods for these people who saved him. And they were made sort of capos of the camp. So they had freedom to move with more movement than the others. And I overheard one night. They were talking about getting a group together to escape, because they had more access to it. One was quite a tall man. I don't remember his name, but I remember he was a good-looking man-- a very good-looking man. And they managed to find some-- break into a warehouse and steal uniforms and guns. As a group, about 10 of them did escape. And I heard that. In those days, although my, whatever job I had-- I also cleaned rooms for German officers. They gave to me extra food, and so forth. When I heard that they was trying to escape and you had to have your own weapons with, I managed to-- to find whether he's kept his revolver in his bag, covered. And the holsters, the German holsters, were very heavy leather, so I only took the revolver. We always worked in twos. I don't remember the guy that I worked with, but he stood watch in the barrack to see if anybody going-- that particular day, luckily, he told me not to come, because he was going away on holiday, or leave, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I managed to get into the barrack, steal his revolver, and hid it in a place wherein I thought, obviously, I'll hear when they're going to escape and I'll join with them. I'm sure they wouldn't want me if I officially, but I thought if I turned up, then-- but unlucky for me, they-- they escaped. But the day when they escaped, they stopped going to work, my group. And I was taken to Tschenstochau, further into another camp. So they escaped, but I, unfortunately, ended up in a-- in another a camp, a labor camp. And did your, is it commandant at the camp ever find-- or sergeant ever find that his pistol had been stolen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, he did. Yes, he did. He came-- what happened was, they had parades. He came back from his holiday, from leave, whatever, and he was in a parade. An officer came to inspect. And they always-- you know, you take  your weapon. You're getting your weapon ready, and so forth. And we were standing from a distance watching in the parade ground. And just before he came to his then, he got himself ready and started-- he looks and he's got nothing there. So I think he was a sergeant, or whatever. He managed to leave and go back to his place and look for it. He didn't find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, he came to the place of work where I work to ask if I seen anybody-- didn't accuse me-- if I'd seen it, because he never thought that I would ever be able of doing that such a thing. Did I see anybody go into the barracks? And I said-- of course, I said, I didn't see anyone. You just told me not to come. He asked did I go to the barracks, did I clean his room. I said, no, you told me you were going away. But I was hit by the capo. I was hit so hard for being accused, or even asked by a German, did I see anybody? Did I do anything, you know. So therefore, the capo gave me a good hiding. But within a day or so, and we-- we weren't allowed to go out anymore to work. And the group escaped, and I was right behind. And what nationality was the capo?

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have been Polish Jewish. Oh, yes. Most capos were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you then went to-- you were then taken to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tschenstochau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Tschenstochau was already under SS. And as we got off the trains, there was a guy with a little-- with a stick, pointing which way to go. And he-- there's a lot of children came from that camp. So he selected the grownups on one side and children other side And he obviously, he could see I wasn't a grownup, and so he pushed me, and he told me to come with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and laid in front of him, kneeled in front of him. I said, I'm a man. I said, ask anybody. I was working at the other camp. I'm 15. And he just happened to push me away. By pushing me away, he pushed me among the grownups. Otherwise, I wouldn't be here to tell you the story. And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I think of it, I still shudder. I just had the vision of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in '44. It must have been '43, '44. I could've been 14-- 13, 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what was it like in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp itself, it wasn't-- it wasn't too bad. Again, it's still Poland, but except it was under SS. I was designated to go to work in a-- in a furniture place. And the guy in charge of the furniture was-- the first time I learned that the deeper-- it's certain rules. The deeper you keep on rubbing it down, the beautiful designs you get in the wood. And we-- we used to make the jewelry boxes or cigarette boxes, that type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were they for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For German officers, or whatever, or sending back home, you know. And I persuaded the guy who was in charge-- he wasn't too bad. He was quite an elderly man. And he liked rabbits, angora rabbits. I remember, white with the red eyes-- beautiful. I persuaded him that I wasn't much use in the workshop, and he needed somebody to look after his rabbits. Now the rabbit hutch was near the kitchen mess-- the officers' mess, or kitchens, you know. And I felt that I could do better there. So eventually, he gave me time to go and look after the rabbits, to clean out the hutch, and give them food. Because the more food they had, that-- I also helped myself, you know. And it went on for-- for a while. And I managed to get keys made to the storeroom. From wire, you make, you know, your different keys. I managed to get into the storeroom to find out what was in there. There was oil-- barrels of oil. So you light a candle, or something, and you get-- you get little drops to come out. Or you take-- you help yourself to a piece of meat. And I did, one day. I helped myself to a chunk of meat, put it under my jacket, or whatever I was wearing. And I was walking through the place to go into the barracks, and the officer with a little-- had a little dog. The dog smelled the meat. So you can imagine what happened after. And I remember this distinctly. He said to me-- he says, you're not going to be punished for stealing. You're going to be punished for being caught. If you're clever enough to steal, you should be clever enough not to be caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what clothes were you wearing when you were in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, normal clothes, whatever we had. It wasn't striped pajama type, so not in the beginning, anyway I don't remember having that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? And sleeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sleeping was in bunks, always-- always bunks with straw. If you were lucky, you had a bit of straw. And-- and in those days, it wasn't the-- the bunks weren't too bad. It was straw. But they were a little bit wider, and you didn't have so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they wooden bunks, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, wooden bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there-- was there any of your family in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the-- eventually, I met my Aunt Tzipporah that was there. And she worked in the laundry room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had she been in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: She must have come at the same time as me. Yes. That was the only transport, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And then from-- when did you leave this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have left  it-- that camp, I must have left '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '44 at Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were you all-- everybody that came to that camp, did you all leave together to go to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no. In fact, my aunt was liberated six months before me in-- in that-- that camp, by the Russians, also by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why were you then--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why. They just came in one day and says, right, that bag is going off, or they called out lists, and you're going off. And that's it. I don't know how many left the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Buchenwald like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Buchenwald-- I-- what can you say with-- it wasn't a-- it wasn't a holiday camp, I can tell you. The bunks that we shared were much more difficult to sleep in than-- than the-- the other previous. Many more people shared those bunks. And we all laid sort of like sardines, you know, one this way, one that way, and even across the legs and across the hands, you know, as many as you could put in. And there was a long-- a long bench-- all long benches, with wooden benches there, sort of in the center with the thing down.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've come to the end now of tape two.
